item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I adore the direction this takes the SPC canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically what he said above me. A more serious (implication-wise) take on the SPC. Having an object that isn’t based on a preexisting SCP or is just kind of silly.

I noticed that it says “title for hiding text” for the “hide text” for one of the collapsibles. I don’t know if that is intentional or not.

item.title: +1 right away
item.content:encoded:
I'm LOVING the implication that the SPC was once a regular organization/alt SCP foundation gone wrong… SPC isn't -J and I'd love to see more about them fleshed out (while still being somewhat humorous?)

It's way too early for me to be writing this comment but I love this right now and want to see more things like this is what I am trying to say.

+ Opinions
- HIDE

I like this, however there's a couple of things I think could be improved:

subconscious shift to a Centre developed anti-selachian ideology.

Centre-developed should probably be hyphenated.

Device is capable of transmitting Meme-3733 over a range of several meters to full global coverage

This sentence feels a little off to me. I think I know what you're trying to say, but something like 'capable of transmitting Meme-3733 over ranges of up to 40,000km' would work better. I don't think the mention of 'several meters' is necessary.

was declared operational, on 8/14/2019.

Comma is not necessary. And on a purely personal note, I much prefer dates formatted as YYYY/MM/DD, as it's the internationally accepted standard (yes, it technically uses hyphens instead, but I much prefer the /)

I'm not sure we ever had control

I prefer the shorter sentence 'I'm not sure we ever were.' as control has already been mentioned, and it's a much more succinct way of saying the same thing.

Overall, I really like the concept. I love when the Center become self-aware, and it's been done in a very good way. The execution is (in my opinion) pretty good too, there were only a few nitpicks.
